observations.managementAction: Management had complied with the recommendations. All unreleased RPT shares for CY 2023 had been fully paid, and the CY 2024 RPT shares for the 46 barangays had been paid on a quarterly basis.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant submit the documents to support the cash advances for Hazard Pay consistent with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and GAM for LGUs to ascertain the validity of the such claims in accordance with AO No. 26.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | The granting of the hazard pay to personnel who reported for work during the implementation of the Enhanced Community Quarantine was in conformity with the Guidelines on the Grant of Covid -19 Hazard Pay through DBM Circular 2020-1 dated March 24, 2021. Since the Local Government Unit is tasked with the direct implementation of the protocols formulated by the IATF, most of the personnel were asked to report for work to perform responsibilities relating to the pandemic especially during the repacking of relief goods, validation and distribution of the Social Amelioration Pension, setting-up of Quarantine facilities and the control checkpoints at the entry points in the Municipality and other related tasks. To alleviate the moral and motivations the personnel in taking the risks during the pandemic, the LGU extended the hazard pay.

The amount granted was on a uniform basis and at a lower rate from the requirements of P500/day as per DBM Circular 2020-1 since the LGU funds will not be sufficient. It was only granted in May 2020 after all the priority programs have been implemented during the ECQ and that the availability of funds have been ascertained. The LGU also extended this pay to barangay officials who also cooperated with the LGU in the maintenance of barangay checkpoints.

Moreover, a minimal amount of P200/day hazard pay was also extended to NGOs who were continuously maintaining the control checkpoints and the various quarantine facilities in the Municipality i.e Mamang Pulis/Aleng Pulis and BAGANI. This kind of support to these personnel is also encouraged by the DILG on which a monthly report is submitted to the MLGOO.

We shall be submitting all the supporting documents (payrolls, and DTRs) for the mentioned cash advances as soon as the Treasury Office submits to the Accounting Office.	We shall also take note your audit observations so as to avoid similar issues in the future.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that a physical count of its inventories shall be conducted on Tumauini Community Hospital and submit a Report on the Physical Count of Inventories as required under Sections 194 and 206 and reflect the same on the face of its financial reports.

We also recommended that the Management provide explanation on the deficiency disclosed on its financial reports and strengthen its internal control to safeguard its assets and its inventory accounts to avoid inaccurate and misleading information.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | The ending balance of the account Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies for Distribution amounting to P562,540.25 represents the ending inventory of various hospital supplies including the procurement of 800 pieces of Antigen Testing Kits amounting to P500,000.00 which were used in January 2021 during the massive testings conducted due to surge of Covid-19 infection in the Municipality. The said inventory was recorded under the General Fund because the funds used to procure the supplies were from the realigned 20% EDF Fund for COVID-19 PPAs. Moreover, the Tumauini Community Hospital has already exhausted its funds as early as 3rd Quarter due to the reduction of the Philhealth Reimbursements in CY 2020. Such funds are normally used for its operation; however, the General Fund subsidized the procurements of its supplies and medicines.

Nevertheless, we’ll take note of the audit observation and reflect the inventories under the TCH books of accounts for CY 2021. The General Services Office had also conducted physical inventories of the said supplies and we’ll submit the same to your office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the creation of Inventory Committee  to conduct the physical inventory taking and to reconcile the results of the count with the property and accounting records.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the prescribed property cards for Local Road Networks and disclose the information for the said property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the  Annex B of COA Circular No.2015-008 dated November 25, 2015;
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and GSO with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | To address the audit observation, an Inventory Committee will be organized by issuing the appropriate Executive Order. Functions and responsibilities of the Committee will be defined and formalized in order to perform a thorough periodic inventory of the properties of the LGU.

The Office of the Municipal Engineer is requested to conduct inventory of all roads with complete description and cost segregation and submit the same to Accounting Office and the GSO for recording and reconciliation purposes.

The Road Network Account in the Financial Statements includes infrastructure projects relating to Local Access Road from CY 2016 to CY 2020. A subsidiary ledger is maintained in the Accounting Office for Property, Plant and Equipment Accounts including Road Network. In connection to this, may we request for your consideration and rest assured that we will comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the entry taking up interest payment as financial expenses but instead, as a debit to appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) account since the loans were used to expand capital/fixed assets to come up with correct asset account balances.
In case of any other factor that affects the amount of interest to be paid due to the bank’s other consideration, the recorded payable will be adjusted accordingly.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020 | The Accounting Office will prepare Journal Entries to capitalize the paid interests of long-term payables amounting to ₱24.836 Million in compliance with the provisions of PD No. 1445 and IPSAS 5 pertaining to Borrowing Costs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up in the books the remaining amount of interest payable incurred in connection with the acquisition of long-term loans from the DBP- City of Ilagan Branch so as not to understate the Payable account. 

Also, it must be taken into consideration that interest expense payable in connection with the acquisition of loans should be taken up in the books and if said funds are used to procure PPEs.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020 | The remaining interest payable on long-term loans will be taken up in the books for CY 2021 and we will submit the necessary JEVs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU submit complete documentation on the granting of honoraria to its personnel involved in government procurement as provided in COA Circular 2012-001. As such, the supporting documents of the claims shall be submitted to ascertain its validity in accordance with DBM Budget Circular No. 2005-4A.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | In order to comply with the requirements of the audit observation, we are submitting herewith the following documents:
	
Executive Order No. 19-A s. 2020 dated July 1, 2020
Minutes of Meetings conducted 
Notice of award to the winning bidder
Attendance Sheet during the conduct of BAC Meeting

Please take note that all of these procurements were conducted through Competitive bidding and fully complied with the requirements of RA 9184 or the Government Procurement Act. 

We would like also to assure that these documents will be attached to the future disbursements pertaining to the granting of honoraria the members of the Bids and awards Committee and personnel involved in the procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC review and strictly implement the procurement processes and to submit complete documentation in accordance with existing laws and regulations to establish the validity of its claims. We also recommend that necessary corrective/explanatory documents for the purchase of office supplies charged against the BGCM shall be submitted.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | We wish to inform your good office that the various emergency procurements relating to the response of LGU Tumauini during the pandemic were conducted in conformity with the provisions of RA 9184. The supplier, Microways Trading, is a reputable business organization whom the LGU had transactions pertaining to the procurement of supplies. During the period of the implementation of the Enhanced Community Quarantine and the rising Covid cases in the Municipality, the LGU made emergency procurements particularly supplies in order to address immediately the needs in our quarantine facilities, check points and other areas. The supplier was able to provide the needed supplies and materials as soon as possible. Please find attached the supplier’s Business permit, Omnibus Sworn statement and Business Registration for your reference. The justification for these procurements was aligned with the declaration of the National Public Health Emergency and the rising numbers of PUMs and PUIs in the Municipality.

We are also submitting the updated Supplemental Procurement Plan which includes the procurements charged from the Bayanihan Grant.

With respect to Check No. 1101232 concerning the procurement of various bond papers, these supplies were used to print the 10,889 Social Amelioration Program Forms. These were procured through emergency for the particular reason that the DSWD and DILG had set the deadline for the distribution of the financial assistance to the beneficiaries as soon as possible during which the ECQ is currently implemented. These were charged from the Bayanihan Grant since this is the available fund for utilization at that time. We believe that the expenditure is a necessary COVID-19 – related PPAs and expenses as these were not used for administrative purposes but to address a necessity brought by the pandemic.

Rest assured that the LGU will continue to implement the procurement processes in accordance with the provisions of the law.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU management strengthen the monitoring of its funds and ensure that the same are managed, expended, and utilized strictly in accordance with applicable rules and regulations. As such, the records and reports prepared and maintained for the Bayanihan Grant released to the LGU shall be submitted to provide clarification on the inconsistencies discovered and ensure that the funds were utilized and disbursed accordingly.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | As per the Municipal Treasurer, the receipt of the Bayanihan Grant amounting to P20,660,601.00 was transferred to our LBP Trust Account instead of opening a new Special Account for the purpose due to the fact that the funds were received in only in April 2020 and the immediate procurement of welfare assistance in the form of packs of rice was needed to be conducted during the implementation of the Enhanced Community Quarantine. Opening of a special account might cause delay in the immediate distribution of relief assistance during the lockdown. 

We would like also to inform you that the Bayanihan Grant was indeed fully utilized as of August 2020 as per reports of the Local Finance Committee and the record of the Accounting Office. A copy of the submitted RCI from the Treasury office was not submitted to Accounting so as to review and reconcile with our records especially with the proper recording of responsibility centers.
	
Rest assured that we will strengthen the monitoring of our Trust Funds and the efficient reconciliation of records. We shall also take not of the requirements for the opening of Special Account for future Trust Funds to conform with the requirements of the circulars.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management emphasize strict compliance with the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 in its procurement process to keep in line with the government’s commitment to promote good governance. Consequently, the lacking and other necessary documents for the P120,000.00 disbursement for consulting services shall be submitted for further verification.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020 | May we seek your consideration on the lacking documents pertaining to the procurement of consultancy services for the formulation of the Forest Land Use Plan amounting to P120,000.00. We shall review the requirements of RA 9144 pertaining to the requirements and procedures in procuring consultancy services to avoid the occurrence of the same observation. We shall be submitting copies of the final outputs of the plans and the other supporting documents for your verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU strengthen its controls on its expenditures for fuel consumptions by properly monitoring its utilization and the submission of the pertinent records and reports. Consequently, necessary supporting documents on the claims shall be submitted to establish its validity and ascertain that the BGCM were strictly utilized in accordance with DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | Please be informed that expenditures for fuel consumption are used for official business purposes only. No request for fuel will be issued unless approved by the LCE or the Municipal Administrator. 

With respect to the fuel used by the service and back-up vehicles of the LCE, these were used during the monitoring of the LCE in various barangays pertaining to the implementation of quarantine protocols, meetings with the barangay officials and the distribution of relief assistance to barangays. In connection to the distribution of Social Amelioration pension, these were used by the personnel and DSWD staff in going to various barangays during the validation and distribution of the cash assistance.

Rest assured that we will take note your audit observation and improve/strengthen our controls in the expenditures for fuel consumption.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with the rules and regulations of government procurement and to accordingly undertake its functions to ensure adherence to the principles of equity, efficiency, and economy in its procurement process.

We also recommended the submission of pertinent documents to provide explanation for the inconsistencies found on the P3,052,000.00 disbursement from the SEF, and full accounting of the 500 reams bond paper.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | The Local Government Unit of Tumauini, through its Bids and Awards Committee, had been complying with the provisions of the Implementing Rules and Regulation (IRR) of the Republic Act No. 9184 or the Government procurement Act in its various procurements particularly the requirement on posting to PhilGeps.

With respect to the change of ABC from P3,017,500.00 to P3,120,000.00, a Bid Supplement Abstract has been uploaded to PhilGeps as a supplement to Invitation to Bid with Reference No. 7158880 published on August 07, 2020 for the purpose of procuring additional 500 reams of long bond papers for the printing of modules of different schools.
The additional 500 reams of long bond paper were not part of the initial requisitions to the schools as these were intended for inventory purposes to be distributed to schools for the printing of modules. The additional supplies were part of the inventory as of December 31, 2020.

Rest assured that the LGU will continue to adhere with the provisions of the Government procurement Act and the efficient management of its supplies.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Management for its consistent efficient delivery of socio-economic developmental projects for the benefits of its constituents. We encourage the Management to continue and remain compliant with the above stated provisions and other rules and regulations regarding the utilization of 20 percent Development Fund. Furthermore, ensure the efficient implementation of all priority development projects and programs under the 20% DF that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development of theMunicipality of Tumauini.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | The utilization of the 20% Economic Development Fund of the Annual Internal Revenue Allotment of the LGU has always passed the criteria of the Seal of Good Local Governance. Since the evaluation of the utilization comes after two years of implementation, we were able to report 100% Utilization Rate of the funds at the time of the assessment. This is attributed to the remarkable efforts of the administration in continually delivering economic development projects in the Municipality.

We thank you for your favorable observation and we’ll make sure that the LGU shall maintain consistency in the implementation of its priority development projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended strict adherence to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for the timely submission of GAD reports to the COA Audit Team. 

We also recommended the Management to ensure that GAD programs, projects, and activities are implemented as planned and to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds to achieve the objectives for which the funds were provided and attain maximum benefits for its intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | The LGU submitted its GAD AR 2020 to COA on February 26, 2021. While we were guided with the abovementioned JMC, we shall take note of the recommendation on the submission of GAD AR to COA, which is within five (5) working days from the end of January of the preceding year as prescribed under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We will assure your good office that we will fully comply with your recommendations especially on the timely submission of GAD AR and implementation of GAD PPAs as planned to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds and in order to attain the objectives and targeted indicators.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the unexpended LDRRMF from prior years shall be included in the LDRRMFIP in accordance with existing rules and regulations to enable the LGU to become a more disaster-resilient Municipality. Consequently, we recommended that the Management provide clarification on the inconsistency discovered to ascertain the reliability of its Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | Please be informed that a meeting was conducted by the LCE with the LDRRM Officer, the MPDC, and the Municipal Accountant to address the audit observation. Among the issues resolved was the timely preparation of LDRRM reports in order to ascertain the unexpended balance in order to incorporate the same the LDRRMFIP of the following year. The LDRRM Offices and the MPDC were instructed to update the Investment Plan for LDRRMF to reflect the changes/updates during the year. For CY 2021, we shall be incorporating the unexpended balance of P742,671.95 in the LDRRMFIP in order to comply with the provisions of the JMC.

With respect to the inconsistency between the Trust Liability – DRRM account and the LDRRM portion of the Notes to FS, this is a result of an entry made to transfer the entire amount to the General Fund for the procurement of welfare assistance during the onslaught of the flooding in the Municipality in November and December 2020. However, during the preparation of the LDRRMF report, the balance of P742,671.95 remained unexpended after taking into account all disbursements and the adjustments to the payables account in the General Fund and no entry was not made to restore the unexpended balance as of December 31, 2020 to the Trust Liability – DRRM Account and reflect the same in the Financial Statements. The Accounting Office had prepared journal entry to reclassify and correct the liability accounts at the beginning of CY 2021. Rest assured that we shall comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management thru  the GSO prepare the inventory of the property belonging to the Municipality, determine the value thereof for purposes of insurance, and inquire from the GSIS of the total premiums of specific properties.

observations.recommendation: 1.8	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant submit the documents to support the cash advances for Hazard Pay consistent with  the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and GAM for LGUs to ascertain the validity of the such claims in accordance with AO No. 26.

2.6	We recommended that a physical count of its inventories shall be conducted on Tumauini Community Hospital and submit a Report on the Physical Count of Inventories as required under Sections 194 and 206 and reflect the same on the face of its financial reports.
2.7	We also recommended that the Management provide explanation on the deficiency disclosed on its financial reports and strengthen its internal control to safeguard its assets and its inventory accounts to avoid inaccurate and misleading information.

3.8	We recommended the creation of Inventory Committee to conduct the physical inventory taking and to reconcile the results of the count with the property and accounting records.
3.9	We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the prescribed property cards for Local Road Networks and disclose the information for the said property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the  Annex B of COA Circular No.2015-008 dated November 25, 2015;
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and GSO with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

4.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the entry taking up interest payment as Financial expenses but instead, as a debit to appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) account since the loans were used to expand capital/fixed assets to come up with correct asset account balances.
4.7	In case of any other factor that affects the amount of interest to be paid due to the bank’s other consideration, the recorded payable will be adjusted accordingly.

5.4	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up in the books the remaining amount of interest payable incurred in connection with the acquisition of long-term loans from the DBP- City of Ilagan Branch so as not to understate the Payable account.
5.5	Also, it must be taken into consideration that interest expense payable in connection with the acquisition of loans should be taken up in the books and if said funds are used to procure PPEs.

6.6	We recommended that the LGU submit complete documentation on the granting of honoraria to its personnel involved in government procurement as provided in COA Circular 2012-001. As such, the supporting documents of the claims shall be submitted to ascertain its validity in accordance with DBM Budget Circular No. 2005-4A.

7.7	We recommended that the BAC review and strictly implement the procurement processes and to submit complete documentation in accordance with existing laws and regulations to establish the validity of its claims. We also recommend that necessary corrective/explanatory documents for the purchase of office supplies charged against the BGCM shall be submitted.

8.6	We recommended that the LGU management strengthen the monitoring of its funds and ensure that the same are managed, expended, and utilized strictly in accordance with applicable rules and regulations. As such, the records and reports prepared and maintained for the Bayanihan Grant released to the LGU shall be submitted to provide clarification on the inconsistencies discovered and ensure that the funds were utilized and disbursed accordingly.

9.8	We recommended that the Management emphasize strict compliance with the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 in its procurement process to keep in line with the government’s commitment to promote good governance. Consequently, the lacking and other necessary documents for the P120,000.00 disbursement for consulting services shall be submitted for further verification.

10.9	We recommended that the LGU strengthen its controls on its expenditures for fuel consumptions by properly monitoring its utilization and the submission of the pertinent records and reports. Consequently, necessary supporting documents on the claims shall be submitted to establish its validity and ascertain that the BGCM were strictly utilized in accordance with DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.

11.8	We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee  strictly comply with the rules and regulations of government procurement and to accordingly undertake its functions to ensure adherence to the principles of equity, efficiency, and economy in its procurement process.
11.9	We also recommended the submission of pertinent documents to provide explanation for the inconsistencies found on the P3,052,000.00 disbursement from the SEF, and full accounting of the 500 reams bond paper.

13.7	We recommended strict adherence to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for the timely submission of GAD reports to the COA Audit Team.
13.8	We also recommended the Management to ensure that GAD programs, projects, and activities are implemented as planned and to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds to achieve the objectives for which the funds were provided and attain maximum benefits for its intended beneficiaries.

14.8	We reiterated our recommendation that the unexpended LDRRMF from prior years shall be included in the LDRRMFIP in accordance with existing rules and regulations to enable the LGU to become a more disaster-resilient Municipality. Consequently, we recommended that the Management provide clarification on the inconsistency discovered to ascertain the reliability of its Financial Statements.

15.11	We recommended that Management thru  the GSO prepare the inventory of the property belonging to the Municipality, determine the value thereof for purposes of insurance, and inquire from the GSIS of the total premiums of specific properties.
15.12 	We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer appropriate funds in the annual budget for the costs of premiums for all insurable properties.

16.7	We recommended to the Management the filling-up of the vacant position of General Services Officer (GSO) and possibly the approval of the appointment in accordance with the qualification cited in Section 490 (a) of RA 7160 and the Civil Service Laws, rules and regulations

Team Rejoinder:

Items number 1, 2, 4, and 11 were competed infrastructure projects in CY 2019 recorded as donations in the LGU-Tumauini book of accounts however, these were not yet officially transferred to the respective Barangays due to absence of 1) Journal Entry Vouchers –to record transfer of completed projects 2) Certificate of Acceptance and MOA between the LGU-Tumauini & concern Barangays- for the proper maintenance or possible repair of the structures and other terms & conditions specify in the MOA.  Also, the absence of Barangay’s book of accounts to record the transfer from LGU-Tumauini as required in PPSAS 1 defines assets as resources controlled by an entity as a result of past events and from which future economic benefits or service potential are expected to flow to the entity. This definition denotes that assets are recognized by the entity that controls them and from which future economic benefits are expected by said entity, which may not necessarily be the owner of such assets. Thus, for the purpose of recognizing and recording the assets under PPSAS, use or benefit and control over those assets take precedence over ownership.

observations.recommendation:
1.6 	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prioritize the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements, prepare a Request Letter address to all depository banks Managers requesting the timely issuances of Bank Statements with all supporting documents after the end of each month for the timely preparation of the BRSs to be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for a timely review and audit. Also, observe strict compliance with the rules and regulations on the preparation and submission of the BRSs within the prescribed period.
1.7	Also, ensure that complete descriptions of reconciling items are provided in the BRSs and duly supported with paid checks, original copies of debit/credit memos, etc. including schedules to facilitate easy verification.

2.4     We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer mark as cancelled all stale/ spoiled checks and return the DVs and supporting documents to the Accounting Department for the preparation of JEVs and recording of transactions as Accounts Payable.  However, in case of stale checks in the custody of the payee, communicate and inform them regarding the stale check with a request to surrender the original copy of the issued check in their possession for the replacement/issuance of a new checks upon submission of the stale checks and shall be marked as cancelled, both the new replacement checks and stale checks shall be reported in the RCI.

3.5	We recommended that the Management transfer their two (2) Current Account Deposit from PNB to any desired/preferred AGDBs government-owned banks preferably the LBP or DBP as prescribed under Section 311 of RA No.7160.

4.7 	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the dropping of cost of old buildings located inside the Tumauini Public Market Compound from the books of accounts to present fairly the correct balances of affected accounts such as Building –Pubic Market and Government Equity accounts.

5.7	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in determining the actual status of all completed and on-going infrastructure projects and to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts. Based on the result of the evaluation and validation,  reflect the accurate balance of the CIP accounts and ensure that all completed and paid projects are immediately classified to their appropriate PPE accounts, and compute and record the appropriate/correct depreciation.

6.7	We recommended that the Management cause the immediate submission of contract documents and all paid DVs with complete supporting documents particularly infrastructure projects which are already delayed/overdue to the Office of the Auditor for us to expedite the conduct of audit, contract review and requesting of inspection of completed infrastructure projects. Moreover, strictly comply with the prescribed period of submission provided under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995

7.6 	We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant maintain an account Due from LGUs-Barangay to record all amounts from the Barangays and conduct immediate review and verification in order to reconcile the reciprocal accounts, Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs- Barangay accounts. Also, maintain barangay book of accounts particularly the accounts Due to and Due from LGU-Tumauini to monitor and record the barangay RPT shares and other transactions.

8.6	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer release the payments of shares w/out delay to respective Barangays on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter as provided under Section 271(d) Chapter 7 of R.A. 7160.

9.8    We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor  coordinate to facilitate the updating of the RPT Account Register and Taxpayers Index Card to come-up with a comprehensive Certified List of the Taxpayers (CLT) which will be submitted to the Municipal Accountant as basis in the setting-up of the RPT and SET Receivables as required under Section 20 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I;

10.7 	We recommended the hiring of additional qualified personnel to be assigned in the Accounting Department, modify/revise of the existing plantilla to include positions, such as Certified Public Accountants and Accounting Graduates to handle accounting and bookkeeping functions, particularly the timely preparation of the Municipality’s reports and barangay accounting reports and related records. Also, personnel handling barangay accounts should be under the supervision of the Municipal Accountant.
10.8 	We also advised the Personnel Selection Board to ensure that the appointee is qualified for the position based on the requirements before issuance of appointment to avoid invalidation of the Civil Service Commission (CSC).
10.9	In addition, formulate plans for a comprehensive training program for all Accounting Personnel to attend related seminars conducted by COA with respect to rules and regulations in accounting, bookkeeping and auditing matters, prepare and implement annual training programs, consistent with its formulated plans.

11.9	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant cause the immediate submission of all financial reports to the Office of the Auditor and strictly comply with the prescribed period of submission provided under Section 70 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I, Items 2.1 and 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 and Section 122 of P.D.No.1445, to ensure timely conduct of review, analysis and validation of Management’s assertions on the fairness of presentation of Financial Statements and to communicate the results thereof to concerned officials.

12.11 We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer comply with the timely submission of reports, records, registries and related documents for us Audit Team to have an ample time to conduct proper audit, review and verification of reports, records, and such other papers or documents as may be required/necessary in the conduct of audit.

13.6	We commended the Management for its very efficient delivery of socio-economic developmental projects for the benefits of its constituents. The Management should continue to remain compliant with Section 287 of RA No. 7160 and Section 5 of JMC No. 2017-1 of the DILG and the DBM, and other rules and regulations regarding the utilization of 20 percent Development Fund. Furthermore, ensure the efficient implementation of all priority development projects and programs under the 20% DF that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development of theMunicipality of Tumauini.

14.5 	We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant record the financial assistance given by the LGU to the various public schools in the Municipality using the account Due from NGAs and require submission of liquidation reports thereof which should be one of the conditions stipulated in the MOA.  The OIC-Municipal Treasurer, being the designated disbursing officer, must be instructed to comply strictly with the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances as stated in COA Circular No. 97-002. Hence, the Management should require him to submit liquidation documents of these cash advances.

15.5	We recommended that the Accounting Department regularly provide copies of RSUDRRMF to the MDRRMO, for them to be well informed of the accurate balance of DRRM Funds available for appropriation as their basis in the preparation of  LDRRMFIP. Also, observe and maintain proper coordination for an  effective implementation of the PPAs pertaining to risk reduction preparedness and mitigation.

16.5	 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and certify the RSUDRRMF to ensure strict compliance on the submission of monthly RSU of the LDRRMF to the Office of the Auditor as mandated under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We advised that the Treasury Department enforce the collection of real property taxes to delinquent tax payers. Apply the collection strategy of collection of the Real Property Tax. The tax starts to accrue on the first of January every year and can be paid in four installments before the office of the local treasurer on or before the end of each quarter. A discount (10% or sometimes 20%) is available to taxpayers who pay the tax in advance while late payments are subject to a two percent (2%) surcharge per month, to a maximum of 36%.

If necessary issue demand letters and apply the remedies as provided in Section 256 of P.D. No.7160.

We acknowledged the effort of the management in lowering the enormous amount real property tax  by P 3.255 million from 21.055 million down to present outstanding balance of P 17.80 million.

observations.recommendation: 1.4   We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer reconcile cashbook balances as against the ledger balances at least once a month or in every quarter to effect timely adjustments in observance of the provisions of Section 181 of the GAAM, Volume I and Section 63 of the LTOM on the required reconciliation of cashbook balances as these are important control tools for cash.

2.8	 Deposit all collections intact to the liquidating officer as required under said law and existing regulations.

3.7	We recommended that the Treasury Department enforce the collection of real property taxes to delinquent tax payers. Apply the collection strategy of collection of the Real Property Tax. The tax starts to accrue on the first of January every year and can be paid in four installments before the office of the local treasurer on or before the end of each quarter. A discount (10% or sometimes 20%) is available to taxpayers who pay the tax in advance while late payments are subject to a two percent (2%) surcharge per month, to a maximum of 36%.
If necessary, issue demand letters and apply the remedies as provided in Section 256 of P.D. No.7160.

4.5	We recommended that the Management:

Maintain Property Ledger Card/Property Cards update adequate accounting and property records and conduct periodic reconciliation between accounting and property records.

5.6	We recommended that Management:
Prepare the necessary Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Waste Material Report (WMR) properly witnessed by the Auditor.
Direct the LGU-Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to transfer affected PPE accounts to Other Asset account in conformity with Section 04 (p), Chapter 2 of the New Government Accounting system (NGAS) for LGUs.

6.5	We recommended that management create an Inventory Committee in-charge in the conduct of inventory taking of all roads under the LGU-Tumauini jurisdiction. Establish and implement effective control measures in the use of government vehicle and fuel consumption as provided in COA Circular No. 77-61

10.8	We recommended that the Municipality should continue properly allocating its collection from additional tax on real property to SEF priority programs/projects/activities as enumerated above so as not to deprive its citizen of the benefits that can be derived therefrom and to maximize the attainment of the purpose for which the fund was created.

b. The Municipal General Services Officer  and Municipal Accountant fast track the identification and listing of buildings, multi-purpose halls, community centers, and other structures from the years 2016 down owned by LGU-Tumauini but used by barangays, for subsequent adjustment.
c. The Municipal General Services Officer  furnish detailed schedules of these structures to the Barangays concerned for reconciliation with the inventory reports (RPCPPE) and the actual accountability of each barangay.
d. Prepare Certificate of Acceptance and MOA between the LGU-Tumauini & concern Barangays for the proper maintenance or possible repair of the structures and other terms & conditions specify in the MOA.

12.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile regularly the balances of payable accounts to the PGI as appearing in the books of the Municipality versus the amount appearing in the books of the Province and make appropriate adjustment.

13.6	We recommended that the Management stop the practice of direct payment payable to the requesting principal/teacher and treated as direct expense instead resort to direct payment to suppliers in case of purchases or cash advance with strict implementation on the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, stated in COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and Sections 89 and 129 of P.D. No.1445.
13.7	The Management should require the Principal/Teacher concerned to immediately liquidate the amount given them by requiring submission of liquidation report/papers to ensure  proper accounting of expenditures incurred,  and refund of unspent balance.

14.7	We  recommended that Management through the GAD Council initiate the creation of GAD database pursuant to Section 37, Rule VI of the IRR of R.A. 9710 and Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

15.2	We  recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of recording transactions charged to the GAD Fund and that the GAD Focal Point coordinate with the Budget Office and Accounting Department and reconcile the draft Accomplishment Report with Budget and Accounting records before it is finalized for submission to offices concerned.

16.4	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  direct  the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately  the above stated reports to the Auditor’s Office, as well as the monthly financial reports and accounts of the LGU regularly, for post audit, in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

17.10	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads involved in development projects to monitor and fast track the implementation and completion of programmed projects and to ensure that funds allocated thereon are utilized for projects identified in the approved AIP, in accordance with the guidelines/policies prescribed under DILG/DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011, as superseded by DILG-DBM JMC 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

18.7	We recommended that Management require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure timely implementation of all the pre-disaster preparedness programs, projects and activities.
18.8	Increase and maximize the utilization of the fund allocated for trainings and the likes, and consider the inclusion in the priority programs of training sessions, simulation exercises, DRRM researches and other similar activities to increase the level of awareness of the community to threats and impacts of all hazards and to equip the community with the necessary skills to cope with the negative impacts of a disaster. Procurement/Acquisition of disaster equipment for disaster response and rescue such as early warning systems, preparedness equipment and other equipage for natural and man-made calamities should also be prioritized .

19.5	We recommended that the Management comply with the submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, as provided in Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in order to monitor compliance on accounting and reporting of the utilization of LDRRMF.

20.5	We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Budget Officer to establish separate Registry of Appropriation, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for the Quick Response Fund (QRF), Maintenance and Other Operating Expense (MOOE) and Capital Outlay (CO) of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LGU discontinue the practice of having one common series of OR for both General Fund and Trust Fund to observe correct numerical sequence of issuances. Issue a separate series of Official Receipts for Trust Fund apart from that of the General Fund upon receipt of revenue/collections.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit immediately the original, duplicate and triplicate copies of cancelled official receipts to form part of the supporting documents of the report of collections.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer account/produce the noted missing 19,512 pieces of accountable forms with money value worth P175,234.00 and the 17,592 pieces accountable forms without money value.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor relieve the involved collecting officers of their duties  as such.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor has issued Demand Letters to involved Collecting Officers including the Municipal Treasurer to produce the missing accountable forms and funds. Also the said Collecting Officers and the Municipal Treasurer have been relieved from their functions and a new set of Liquidating Officer and Collecting Officers were also installed in order to strengthen the Internal Control in handling the cash collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management address the identified internal control weaknesses on handling and reporting cash to achieve the objectives of a sound internal control system.
observations.managementAction: Internal control in handling cash collections has been strengthened. All documents are verifiable anytime.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer enforce collection of the delinquent real property taxes by conducting tax campaign and granting incentives or relief to taxpayers thru' compromise agreement with the authority of the Sangguniang Bayan, otherwise, avail of the remedies provided in Section 256 to 260 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: To enforce RPT collection, the Office of the Municipal treasurer started to send Notice of delinquency to all delinquent taxpayers. This resulted to a P2,035,548.13 increase in the collections compared to prior year. observations.managementAction: This recommendation has been complied with. The balance of Cash Local Treasury as of December 31, 2017 is already reconciled with the balances in the Cash Books of the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the practice of disbursements through reimbursement be refrained through issuing a check payable to the creditor/payee.
observations.managementAction: We have implemented the provisions of Section 93 of PD No. 1445 which require that payments of expenses should be made to supplier instead on a reimbursement basis. Only expenses for Travelling and Petty expenses are allowed for reimbursement.
observations.reason: However, in some few cases, reimbursements are necessary specially when there is a need to immediately pay/settle an obligation in order to avoid delay in the delivery of services. Moreover, reimbursements by the Municipal Mayor are also necessary in the discharge of his official duties and functions especially during his official travels to partner Government Agencies of the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We   recommended   that management follow strictly the provisions of Section 13, Chapter 3, Volume I of NGAS Manual for LGUs, that one depository account be maintained per fund. The Treasurer and Accounting Office should likewise maintain subsidiary ledgers for the various sub funds in the General fund and these suffice to provide information relative thereto.
